figures.name: Numi-Torum (The Sky Father)
figures.gloss: The supreme creator and sky father who dwells in the luminous upper world. He establishes the cosmic order and oversees the earth from afar, but finds the noise and impurity of humanity offensive, remaining utterly removed from direct intervention.
figures.facet: His remoteness is so absolute that humans cannot pray to him directly; his blessings must be mediated. He literally lowered his offspring to earth in silver cradles to distance himself from the chaos of terrestrial life.
figures.provenance: Mansi epic prayers and folklore; documented by B. Munkácsi and V. N. Chernetsov.

figures.name: Mir-susne-khum (World-Surveyor-Man)
figures.gloss: The seventh and youngest son of Numi-Torum, who rides across the sky on a winged white horse. He serves as the vital intermediary between the high heavens and the human realm, actively protecting mankind and delivering their prayers.
figures.facet: He acts as a literal cosmic patrolman. He physically surveys the taiga and river networks daily, transforming the abstract, distant will of the sky god into concrete, localized protection for hunters and fishers.
figures.provenance: Extensively detailed in the 'Vogul Népköltési Gyűjtemény' (B. Munkácsi); A. Kannisto.

figures.name: Kaltash-Ekva (Golden-Haired Mother)
figures.gloss: The supreme earth goddess and sister-wife of Numi-Torum, cast down from heaven to dwell in a sacred grove. She governs childbirth, fertility, and the strict allocation of human destinies.
figures.facet: Her administration of fate is highly bureaucratic and arboreal. She physically registers the exact lifespan and destiny of every human soul on a golden birch-bark tally, making destiny a readable, material record.
figures.provenance: Ob-Ugric creation myths; V. N. Chernetsov.

figures.name: Kuly-Otyr (Lord of the Underworld)
figures.gloss: The master of the underworld, disease, and the dead. He is the structural rival of Numi-Torum, ruling from a dark, frozen realm situated deep beneath the earth and far to the north.
figures.facet: His domain is explicitly mapped onto Siberian hydrology. The underworld is not a fiery abyss, but a literal geographical destination reached by traveling continuously downstream to the icy, hostile mouth of the Ob River.
figures.provenance: V. N. Chernetsov; M. Hoppál, 'Uralic Mythologies'.

figures.name: Pupi-Yik (The Bear Feast)
figures.gloss: The paramount, multi-day ritual complex following a bear hunt, featuring hundreds of sacred songs and masked dramas. It is designed to entertain, honor, and safely return the slain bear's soul to its heavenly origin.
figures.facet: The rite is fundamentally a hostage negotiation and apology. The bear is understood not as an animal, but as a divine, exiled son of the sky god; humans must convince him he is an honored guest so he will report favorably to heaven.
figures.provenance: E. Schmidt; A. Kannisto, 'Materialien zur Mythologie der Wogulen'.

figures.name: Por and Mos (The Dual Phratries)
figures.gloss: The two strictly exogamous phratries that divide Ob-Ugric society. The Mos are spiritually aligned with the sky, light, and Numi-Torum, while the Por are tied to the earth, the underworld, and the sacred bear.
figures.facet: Cosmic dualism here is biologically enforced. Because one must marry outside their phratry, the grand metaphysical tension between the celestial and terrestrial realms is literally resolved in every marriage bed and family unit.
figures.provenance: Structural ethnographies by V. N. Chernetsov and P. Hajdú.

figures.name: Is and Lil (The Bipartite Soul)
figures.gloss: The dual-soul system inherent to all humans. The 'is' (shadow soul) is tethered to the physical form and descends to the underworld at death, whereas the 'lil' (breath or hair soul) remains above to be reincarnated.
figures.facet: Reincarnation is strictly a closed, local loop. The breath-soul is physically anchored in the hair and is exclusively reborn into a newborn of the exact same family lineage, making ancestors and descendants parts of one continuous metabolic cycle.
figures.provenance: V. N. Chernetsov, 'Concepts of the Soul Among the Ob-Ugrians'.

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The eternal structural opposition between the remote luminous sky father and the hydrologically downstream lord of disease.
Intra-tradition bond: The withdrawn creator and his active, mobile son who executes his will and mediates with humanity.
Intra-tradition bond: The Mos phratry traces its direct spiritual lineage and social obligations to the sky father.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A well-documented historical borrowing; the Ob-Ugric figure assimilated profound solar and horse-riding attributes from ancient Indo-Iranian steppe contacts with Mithra.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The overarching, often distant sky and weather deity shared across Finno-Ugric cosmological frameworks.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme, blue-sky creator of the Eurasian expanse.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Siberian lord of the underworld, situated conceptually 'downward' and serving as the structural counterpart to the sky creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An underworld conceptualized not as fire, but as a freezing, hydrologically northern realm of the dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The fierce, immanent Siberian earth mother who safeguards childbirth and the physical span of life.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A goddess who not only gives life but physically dictates and records the precise lifespan and destiny of newborns.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A central game animal elevated to divine status, whose death requires an elaborate, respectful metabolic exchange to ensure cosmic continuity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel: intense, multi-day communal rituals centered around a sacred animal's potency to repair the social and cosmic fabric.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A comprehensive bipartite cosmology that maps fundamental elemental oppositions directly onto social and practical life.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A specific soul-part that ensures reincarnation strictly within a given family lineage, effectively recycling ancestral identity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The physical, shadow or shade component of the soul that departs to the afterlife, distinct from the reincarnating essence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial, luminous sky-father archetype.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A magical, mobile Finno-Ugric culture hero who directly intervenes on behalf of his people.

proposedArchetypes.name: Divine Quarry
proposedArchetypes.gloss: An animal that is physically hunted but conceptually treated as a divine, exiled, or voluntary emissary from the gods, requiring elaborate posthumous hospitality.
proposedArchetypes.reason: Necessary to distinguish from a 'master-of-animals' (who owns the game). This covers the Ainu bear, the San eland, and the Ob-Ugric bear, where the prey itself is a visiting god.

proposedArchetypes.name: Dual Soul
proposedArchetypes.gloss: A bipartite human soul structure cleanly divided into a mortal or shadow aspect that travels to the underworld, and a breath or name aspect that reincarnates.
proposedArchetypes.reason: Crucial for mapping circumpolar and Siberian psychologies (like the Inuit Atiq/Tarniq and the Ob-Ugric Is/Lil) which do not fit the monolithic Abrahamic soul nor Buddhist anatman.
